_comment: F6 SRP-6a cross-implementation known-answer test (KAT).
FIXED inputs shared by the Go server test (usbvault-server/internal/auth/srp_test.go,
TestSRPInteropKAT) and the Rust client test (usbvault-crypto/src/srp_client.rs,
tests::srp_interop_kat). Both implementations MUST independently reproduce the
identical expected_* values below using the ONE canonical RFC 5054-style convention:
Group  = RFC 7919 ffdhe3072, g = 2, len(N) = 384 bytes.
PAD(x) = left-zero-pad big-endian to 384 bytes.
Hash   = SHA-256.
k  = H(PAD(N) || PAD(g))
A  = g^a mod N            (client public ephemeral)
B  = (k*v + g^b) mod N    (server public ephemeral)
v  = g^x mod N            (verifier; x is injected directly here, NOT password-derived,
so the KAT does not depend on the Argon2id x-derivation)
u  = H(PAD(A) || PAD(B))
S_client = (B - k*g^x)^(a + u*x) mod N
S_server = (A * v^u)^b mod N   (MUST equal S_client)
K  = H(PAD(S))
M1 = H(PAD(A) || PAD(B) || K)
M2 = H(PAD(A) || M1 || K)
All inputs/outputs are lowercase hex, big-endian, NO 0x prefix.
The expected_* fields are the locked contract: any divergence between Go and Rust
(or a regression) makes both KAT tests fail.
_inputs_note: x_hex is a 384-byte (768 hex char) value 0x...07 (i.e. the integer 7), padded only for
readability; tests parse it as a big integer so leading zeros are irrelevant.
a = 3, b = 5, x = 7 are tiny FIXED scalars chosen so the KAT is fully deterministic with
NO RNG. They exercise the full modular-exponentiation + padded-hash pipeline against the
real 3072-bit N. (Tiny exponents are fine for a KAT: it tests byte-level interop of the
hashing/padding convention, not ephemeral-key entropy, which is enforced elsewhere.)

_fill_on_first_green_run: These are the locked cross-language outputs. They are derived purely by deterministic
math (modpow + SHA-256 over 384-byte padded buffers) from the fixed inputs above, so
the Go and Rust runs MUST produce byte-identical values. Populate each field with the
hex emitted by the first passing run (the Go and Rust tests print them); thereafter both
tests assert against these constants. Until populated, both tests fall back to asserting
Go-path == Rust-path equivalence via the shared-secret/M1/M2 round-trip (see test code).
